title: Offside Goal — VAR Calibration Error
summary: A goal was incorrectly ruled out for offside due to improper VAR calibration lines drawn from the wrong frame.
match: Japan vs Costa Rica — Group E
description: Japan scored what appeared to be a legitimate goal, but after a lengthy VAR review, the goal was disallowed for offside. The semi-automated offside technology (SAOT) drew the offside line using a frame that analysts later argued was not the exact moment the ball was played. The discrepancy was a matter of a single frame (approx. 0.04 seconds), but the attacker's position changed measurably between the correct frame and the frame used. The goal's disallowance proved decisive as the match ended in a draw, affecting qualification from the group stage. This incident highlighted ongoing concerns about the precision and transparency of semi-automated offside technology.

images.caption: The offside line drawn by VAR was later disputed for using the wrong frame.

rules.explanation: Law 11 defines the offside position and offence. A player is in an offside position if 'any part of the head, body or feet is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent.' The semi-automated offside technology assists with this determination, but the technology must accurately identify the exact moment the ball is played. Incorrect frame selection violates the precision standard required by Law 11.

The VAR Protocol states that technology may be used to assist with 'goal/no goal' decisions including offside. However, the protocol requires 'clear and obvious error' for overturning on-field decisions, and the technology must be applied accurately. Drawing the SAOT line from the wrong kick frame violates the protocol's accuracy requirements and undermines the integrity of the review process.

varOutcome: Goal disallowed after VAR review — frame selection error identified post-match.
